paper: Kwapisz, Weiss, and Moore, Activity Recognition using Cell Phone Accelerometers
exact_task_definition: 6 activities: walking, jogging, walking upstairs, walking downstairs, sitting, standing; 29 users
sensor_setup: Android phone triaxial accelerometer sampled around 20 Hz
evaluation_protocol: 10-fold cross-validation over examples/windows as reported in the original benchmark literature
model_family: J48 decision tree, logistic regression, multilayer perceptron
feature_representation: Handcrafted time-domain window features from x/y/z accelerometer signals
reported_metrics: Best reported accuracy about 91.7% with multilayer perceptron
main_limitation: Evaluation is easier than unseen-subject testing and uses only one phone accelerometer sensor
fairness_note: Useful historical baseline, but not apples-to-apples with subject-wise WISDM 2019 results
actionable_takeaway: A scoped 6-class HAR task is a standard starting point, but modern work should separate subjects before evaluation.

paper: Weiss et al., WISDM Smartphone and Smartwatch Activity and Biometrics Dataset
exact_task_definition: 18 activities collected from 51 subjects; activities include walking, jogging, stairs, sitting, standing, eating, typing, brushing teeth, kicking, and other daily activities
sensor_setup: Phone and smartwatch accelerometer plus gyroscope streams, sampled at 20 Hz
evaluation_protocol: Dataset descriptor; provides raw sensor data and 10-second transformed examples rather than one canonical modeling split
model_family: Dataset release, not a single model benchmark
main_limitation: Because it is a dataset descriptor, downstream papers choose different subsets, sensors, windows, and split protocols
fairness_note: This is the right source for defining a priori 18-class and sensor-ablation experiments, not for claiming model superiority
actionable_takeaway: Use the full 18-class task as the hard benchmark and make all subset choices explicit before looking at test results.

paper: Peppas et al., A Real-Time Activity Recognition Framework Using Smartphone Accelerometers
exact_task_definition: Classic WISDM activity-recognition task using smartphone accelerometer activities such as walking, jogging, stairs, sitting, and standing
model_family: CNN-style deep model compared with CNN, LSTM, and BiLSTM variants
feature_representation: Forty handcrafted time-domain accelerometer features used by the real-time framework
main_limitation: Phone-only and old/scoped task, so it does not test smartwatch fusion or the 18-class WISDM 2019 benchmark
fairness_note: One of the cleaner comparisons because it uses user-independent evaluation
actionable_takeaway: A well-designed classical or compact deep model can be strong under subject-wise evaluation; do not assume raw deep learning automatically wins.

paper: Oluwalade et al., Human Activity Recognition using Deep Learning Models on Smartphones and Smartwatches Sensor Data
exact_task_definition: 15-class task after dropping Eating Pasta, Eating Soup, and Kicking a Soccer Ball because of missing sensor readings
sensor_setup: Sensor ablations include watch accelerometer, phone accelerometer, phone accelerometer plus gyroscope, and watch accelerometer plus gyroscope; 5-second windows with 100 time steps
feature_representation: Raw sequence windows shaped as 100x3 for one sensor or 100x6 for paired accelerometer plus gyroscope
reported_metrics: Best watch-accelerometer results around CNN 94.47% accuracy, ConvLSTM 94.29%, BiLSTM 93.49%, LSTM 91.42%
main_limitation: Random splitting can mix subjects and near-neighbor windows, inflating performance relative to unseen-subject testing
fairness_note: Good for sensor/model inspiration, not a fair target number for subject-wise experiments
actionable_takeaway: Watch accelerometer can be highly informative, and CNN/LSTM/ConvLSTM are reasonable deep baselines to reproduce under a stricter split.

paper: Tan et al., Human Activity Recognition Using a Hybrid Neural Network and a Regularized Extreme Learning Machine
wisdm_version_or_role: WISDM benchmark using windowed activity samples
exact_task_definition: 18 WISDM activities; samples are constructed from 100-row windows and windows containing multiple activities are removed
sensor_setup: Three-axis acceleration windows are emphasized in the paper's WISDM setup
evaluation_protocol: Training/testing split after window construction, reported as 34,316 training samples and 14,706 testing samples
model_family: Hybrid CNN, BiGRU, squeeze-and-excitation, and regularized extreme learning machine; compared with LSTM, GRU, CNN hybrids
feature_representation: Raw 100x3 time-series windows with deep representation learning
main_limitation: Very high performance is likely tied to an easier split protocol; subject leakage is not ruled out
fairness_note: Do not compare this number directly against subject-wise results
actionable_takeaway: Removing mixed-activity windows is methodologically useful, but high deep-learning scores need strict split auditing.

paper: Alexan et al., Human Activity Recognition Using Machine Learning and Wearable Sensors
exact_task_definition: 7 activities selected from the first five users: walking, jogging, stairs, sitting, standing, brushing teeth, and kicking a soccer ball
sensor_setup: Smartwatch accelerometer and gyroscope axes converted into 2D plot/image representations
evaluation_protocol: Subset split not presented as subject-wise; only first five users were used
model_family: Random forest, SVM, kNN, CNN, VGG16, MobileNetV2, LSTM
feature_representation: Sensor traces converted to 2D image-like plots for several models
reported_metrics: Random forest reported 91.18% accuracy and 91.26% F1; CNN 89.42% accuracy; LSTM 83.18% accuracy
main_limitation: Small user subset and image conversion make it hard to generalize to the full WISDM population
fairness_note: Useful as an exploratory representation paper, not as a rigorous unseen-subject benchmark
actionable_takeaway: Classical random forest remains competitive; image-style conversion is less central than robust subject-wise evaluation.

paper: Turdalyuly et al., Wearable Sensor-Free Adult Physical Activity Monitoring Using Smartphone IMU Signals and Deep Learning
exact_task_definition: 6 broad categories remapped a priori from WISDM 2019: stationary, walking, stairs, sport, household, and eating
sensor_setup: Phone accelerometer, phone gyroscope, watch accelerometer, watch gyroscope, and multimodal fusion setups
model_family: CNN1D, LSTM, GRU, BiLSTM, and fusion neural models
feature_representation: Raw IMU sequences with single-sensor, smartphone-fusion, and phone-plus-watch fusion variants
reported_metrics: Best macro F1 reported around 46.0% for smartphone channels; smartphone accelerometer plus gyroscope fusion around 45.2%; adding watch streams around 43.4%
main_limitation: The remapped broad task is not identical to the original 18-class task, and performance is much lower under realistic cross-subject evaluation
fairness_note: Closest fairness match among recent work because it emphasizes grouped subject evaluation
actionable_takeaway: Subject-wise WISDM is much harder than random-split papers suggest; fusion should be tested, not assumed to help.
